ground_truth.title: Device Clock Generation
ground_truth.main_content: After building a CPU, utilities for handling bus interconnects, several DMAs and memory controllers, I often find my time focused on building interfaces between designs and external peripherals. This seems to be where most of the business has landed for me. Often, these peripherals require a clock output, coming from the design, and so I'd like to spend some time describing how to generate such a "device" clock. The next design I worked with was a NAND flash design. NAND flash can be a challenge, since the protocol requires you to start at a slow frequency and only after you bring up the connection are you allowed to change to a faster frequency. Many protocols require data transitions on both edges of the clock, a protocol often known as "Dual Data Rate" (DDR). Unlike the other designs above, these often require a clock that is 90 degrees offset from the data so that each clock transition takes place in the middle of each data valid window, rather than on the edges of the window. All this is to say that our goal today will be to create a divided clock using digital, rather than analog, logic. The basic solution is to return to the rules, and so avoid all transitions on the device clock edge at all. Instead, we'll continue to transition on our source clock and then use either an ODDR or an OSERDES to generate the final outgoing clock. In the meantime, we'll treat the newly generated device clock as a traditional logic signal rather than a "clock" within our design. That is, we'll let it be and remain logic. There are four phases to the outgoing clock, either 0011 or 0110. Key features include no need for clock domain crossings, the ability to gate the clock at will, controlled frequency changes, and although the clock is generated in logic, it doesn't trigger any logic.
